skills.prompt: This topic covers variables and assignments for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-1.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that variables are named storage locations that hold values which can change during program execution. Key concepts: assignment uses the arrow operator in pseudocode (a <- 5) or equals sign in most languages; the right side is evaluated first, then stored in the left-side variable; variables can store numbers, strings, and booleans; variable names should be meaningful and descriptive. Students should trace through sequential assignment statements, predict variable values after a series of operations, and understand that assignment overwrites the previous value. Swapping two variables requires a temporary variable. Important: assignment is not the same as mathematical equality (a <- a + 1 is valid in programming). Vocabulary: variable, assignment, value, expression, initialization, overwrite, swap, temporary variable, identifier.

This topic covers data abstraction for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-1.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that data abstraction manages complexity by grouping related data and hiding implementation details. Key concepts: lists (arrays) store ordered collections of elements accessed by index; data abstraction allows programs to work with complex data without knowing its internal representation; abstract data types define what operations can be performed without specifying how they are implemented. Students should create and use lists to store multiple related values, understand that abstraction reduces complexity and makes programs easier to develop and maintain, and recognize that choosing appropriate data representations affects program efficiency and clarity. Connect to the Create Performance Task where students must demonstrate data abstraction. Vocabulary: abstraction, list, array, index, data structure, implementation, interface, encapsulation, complexity management.

This topic covers mathematical expressions for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand how to use arithmetic operators in programs to compute values. Key concepts: standard operators include addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), division (/), and modulo (MOD); the order of operations follows standard mathematical precedence; integer division vs. floating-point division; the MOD operator returns the remainder of division and is useful for determining even/odd, cycling through values, and extracting digits. Students should evaluate expressions step by step, predict the result of complex arithmetic expressions, and use parentheses to control evaluation order. Common applications: converting units, computing averages, calculating percentages, and determining divisibility. Vocabulary: arithmetic operator, expression, operand, precedence, MOD, integer division, remainder, evaluation, order of operations.

This topic covers strings for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that strings are ordered sequences of characters used to represent text data. Key concepts: strings can be created using quotes; concatenation joins two strings together; the length of a string is the number of characters it contains; individual characters can be accessed by their position (index); substrings extract a portion of a string. Students should manipulate strings using concatenation, determine string length, extract characters and substrings, and understand that strings are a fundamental data type for processing text input, displaying output, and working with textual data. Common operations: searching for a character or substring, comparing strings, and building strings from parts. Vocabulary: string, character, concatenation, length, index, substring, text, sequence, string operations, comparison.

This topic covers boolean expressions for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand that boolean expressions evaluate to either true or false and are used to make decisions in programs. Key concepts: relational operators (=, !=, <, >, <=, >=) compare values; logical operators (AND, OR, NOT) combine boolean expressions; truth tables show all possible outcomes for logical operators; AND is true only when both operands are true; OR is true when at least one operand is true; NOT reverses the value. Students should build and evaluate compound boolean expressions, construct truth tables, and apply De Morgan's Laws (NOT(a AND b) = NOT a OR NOT b). Boolean expressions are the foundation of conditionals and loop conditions. Vocabulary: boolean, true, false, relational operator, logical operator, AND, OR, NOT, truth table, De Morgan's Laws, compound expression.

This topic covers conditionals for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.G, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand that conditional statements allow programs to make decisions and execute different code paths based on boolean conditions. Key concepts: IF statements execute a block only when the condition is true; IF-ELSE provides two-way branching; the condition is a boolean expression; conditionals enable programs to respond differently to different inputs and situations. Students should trace through conditional logic, predict which code block executes for given values, and write conditionals to implement decision-making. Common applications: input validation, categorizing data, game logic, and user interface responses. Understanding conditionals is essential for the Create Performance Task. Vocabulary: conditional, IF, IF-ELSE, condition, boolean expression, branching, selection, control flow, decision, code path.

This topic covers nested conditionals for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.I, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that conditionals placed inside other conditionals create multi-level decision structures. Key concepts: nested conditionals check additional conditions within an already-selected branch; they enable more complex decision-making than simple IF-ELSE; the order of conditions matters; deeply nested conditionals can often be simplified using compound boolean expressions (AND, OR). Students should trace through nested IF-ELSE structures, determine which branch executes for various input values, and recognize when nested conditionals can be refactored into simpler equivalent forms. Common use cases: grading systems with multiple criteria, multi-step validation, and decision trees. Best practice: avoid excessive nesting (more than 3 levels) for readability. Vocabulary: nested conditional, inner condition, outer condition, decision tree, branching depth, refactoring, compound condition.

This topic covers iteration (loops) for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.J, Unit 3, Topic 3.8). Students should understand that iteration allows a program to repeat a block of code multiple times. Key concepts: REPEAT n TIMES loops execute the body exactly n times; REPEAT UNTIL (condition) loops execute until the condition becomes true; loop body is the code that repeats; iteration is essential for processing lists, searching for values, and performing repetitive computations. Students should trace through loops step by step, count iterations, predict output, and identify infinite loops (where the condition never becomes true). Common patterns: accumulating a total, counting items that meet a condition, processing each element in a list. Iteration combined with conditionals enables powerful algorithms. Vocabulary: iteration, loop, REPEAT, REPEAT UNTIL, loop body, infinite loop, iteration count, accumulator, counter, termination condition.

This topic covers developing algorithms for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.L, Unit 3, Topic 3.9). Students should understand that an algorithm is a finite set of instructions that accomplishes a specific task. Key concepts: algorithms can be expressed in natural language, pseudocode, or flowcharts; sequencing, selection (conditionals), and iteration (loops) are the three building blocks of all algorithms; existing algorithms can be combined to create new ones; there can be multiple correct algorithms for the same problem. Students should design algorithms to solve problems, express them in pseudocode, trace through them to verify correctness, and compare different algorithmic approaches. Key understanding: algorithms must be precise enough that anyone (or any computer) following the steps will get the same result. Vocabulary: algorithm, pseudocode, flowchart, sequencing, selection, iteration, step-by-step, correctness, efficiency, deterministic.

This topic covers lists for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-1.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.10). Students should understand that lists are ordered collections of elements that allow programs to store and process multiple related values. Key concepts: list elements are accessed by index (1-based in AP pseudocode); common operations include appending (adding to the end), inserting at a position, removing an element, and accessing/updating by index; lists can store any data type including numbers, strings, and other lists. Traversing a list with a loop processes each element; FOR EACH item IN list iterates through all elements. Students should implement algorithms that search lists, compute aggregates (sum, average, min, max), filter elements, and modify list contents. Lists are a fundamental data abstraction for the Create Performance Task. Vocabulary: list, element, index, append, insert, remove, traverse, FOR EACH, length, data abstraction, collection.

This topic covers binary search for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.P, Unit 3, Topic 3.11). Students should understand that binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding an element in a sorted list by repeatedly dividing the search space in half. Key concepts: binary search requires the list to be sorted; it compares the target to the middle element and eliminates half the remaining elements; it has O(log n) efficiency, meaning it can search 1 billion items in about 30 steps; contrast with linear search which checks every element and has O(n) efficiency. Students should trace through binary search step by step, determine the maximum number of comparisons needed, and understand why sorted data enables this efficiency. Important: binary search cannot be used on unsorted data. This illustrates how algorithm choice dramatically affects performance. Vocabulary: binary search, sorted list, midpoint, search space, logarithmic, efficiency, linear search, comparison, divide and conquer.

This topic covers developing procedures for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.13). Students should understand how to define reusable procedures (functions) that encapsulate a sequence of instructions. Key concepts: a procedure has a name, optional parameters, and a body of instructions; procedures with a RETURN statement send a value back to the caller; procedures promote code reuse and reduce duplication; procedural abstraction hides implementation details so the caller only needs to know what the procedure does, not how. Students should write procedures that accept parameters and return values, understand that parameters act as placeholders for arguments, and recognize that breaking a program into procedures makes it more readable and maintainable. This is critical for the Create Performance Task which requires a student-developed procedure. Vocabulary: procedure, function, parameter, argument, return value, abstraction, modularity, code reuse, encapsulation.

This topic covers calling procedures for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.12). Students should understand how to use (call) existing procedures and interpret their behavior. Key concepts: calling a procedure executes its instructions; arguments are the values passed to parameters during the call; the return value (if any) can be stored in a variable or used in an expression; built-in procedures and library procedures provide pre-written functionality. Students should trace through procedure calls, determine what values are returned, understand that the calling code resumes after the procedure finishes, and distinguish between procedures that return values and those that perform actions (void). Properly calling procedures requires matching the number and type of arguments to the parameters. Vocabulary: procedure call, argument, parameter, return value, built-in procedure, library, execution flow, caller, invocation.

This topic covers random values for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.15). Students should understand how programs generate and use random numbers to model unpredictable behavior. Key concepts: RANDOM(a, b) generates a random integer from a to b inclusive; random values introduce variability into programs; randomness is used in simulations, games, security (encryption keys), and sampling. Students should use random values to make programs behave differently each time they run, generate random selections from a range, and understand that computer-generated random numbers are actually pseudo-random (generated by deterministic algorithms). Applications: simulating dice rolls, coin flips, random sampling from a population, generating test data, and adding unpredictability to games. Vocabulary: random, pseudo-random, RANDOM function, range, simulation, variability, seed, unpredictability, uniform distribution.

This topic covers undecidable problems for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-4.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.18). Students should understand that some problems cannot be solved by any algorithm, no matter how powerful the computer. Key concepts: a decidable problem has an algorithm that always produces a correct yes/no answer in finite time; an undecidable problem has no algorithm that can solve all instances correctly; the Halting Problem (determining whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever) is the classic example of an undecidable problem, proven by Alan Turing. Students should understand that undecidability is a fundamental limit of computing, not a matter of insufficient technology; distinguish undecidable problems from problems that are merely hard or slow to solve; and recognize that some instances of an undecidable problem may be solvable even though a general solution is impossible. Vocabulary: decidable, undecidable, Halting Problem, algorithm, solvable, Turing, computability, theoretical limits.

This topic covers algorithmic efficiency for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-4.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.17). Students should understand that different algorithms for the same problem can have vastly different execution times as input size grows. Key concepts: efficiency is measured by how the number of steps grows relative to input size; reasonable time algorithms (polynomial: linear, quadratic) can solve problems practically; unreasonable time algorithms (exponential, factorial) become impractical for large inputs; heuristic approaches provide approximate solutions to problems that cannot be solved efficiently. Students should compare linear search O(n) vs. binary search O(log n), understand that some problems have no known efficient solution, and recognize that a problem solvable in reasonable time on one input size may become unreasonable for larger inputs. Vocabulary: efficiency, reasonable time, unreasonable time, polynomial, exponential, heuristic, optimization, input size, scalability.

This topic covers simulations for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.F, Unit 3, Topic 3.16). Students should understand that simulations are programs that model real-world phenomena to test hypotheses and make predictions. Key concepts: simulations use abstractions to simplify complex systems (not every detail is modeled); they often use random numbers to introduce variability; simulations enable experiments that would be too costly, dangerous, or time-consuming in the real world; the accuracy of a simulation depends on how well the model represents reality. Students should understand trade-offs in simulation design (detail vs. speed), recognize that simulations cannot perfectly predict real-world outcomes, and identify what simplifications were made. Examples: weather forecasting, traffic flow, spread of disease, physics simulations. Vocabulary: simulation, model, abstraction, hypothesis, random, variability, prediction, simplification, trade-off, real-world modeling.

This topic covers libraries for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.14). Students should understand that libraries are collections of pre-written procedures that programmers can use to extend the functionality of their programs. Key concepts: libraries save development time by providing tested, reusable code; APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) define how to interact with a library's procedures; using libraries is a form of procedural abstraction (you use the functionality without needing to understand the implementation); libraries exist for math operations, data visualization, web development, machine learning, and more. Students should understand how to import and use library procedures, read API documentation, and recognize that libraries enable programmers to build on others' work. Open-source libraries are freely available and community-maintained. Vocabulary: library, API, import, module, package, documentation, open source, reusable code, procedural abstraction, dependency.
